text:
At the 4th Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4), a high-resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS) was used to investigate gas phase emission chemistry from a wide variety of biomass fuels including peat, agricultural residues, and domestic cooking fires. Using a hybrid calibration method based on gas standards and composition-sensitive, mass-dependent sensitivity curves, quantitative non-methane organic compound (NMOC) measurements were achieved. These measurements were then combined with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy to positively or tentatively identify between 80-96% of the measured NMOCs (by mass) for a variety of fuel types including many of higher molecular weight.

Here we present a new set of emission factors (EFs) for the major biomass energy sources worldwide, and for a wide range of chemicals some of which are rarely or never measured before. These include furans, phenolics and other aromatic hydrocarbons precursors of secondary organic aerosol (SOA). The majority of the emissions are identified as oxygenated Non-Methane Organic Compounds (NMOCs). The EFs for the different cooking appliances show that the older three-stone open air designs have higher EFs than the modern improved stoves. In addition to the already known species, we also identified and quantified several little studied nitrogen-containing organic compounds (NOCs) that constituted up to 8.7% of the fuel nitrogen content, and may therefore have a large impact on the newly formed particles produced in biomass smoke plumes.
explanation: The passage contains detailed, domain-specific methods and measurements that align with real biomass emission studies; such specificity reduces AI-detection risk but doesn't rule it out.
